section.title: The Lord Says to My Lord
section.sectionRationale: The most quoted Old Testament passage in the New Testament, this royal psalm reveals a coming King who is also David's Lord, seated at God's own right hand and declared a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek, uniting throne and altar in a single figure.
section.thesis: God Himself installs a King who is also a Priest forever, One greater than David who reigns at the LORD's right hand and mediates forever after the order of Melchizedek, a figure the New Testament identifies as Jesus Christ.
section.frameNote: Two matched movements built around divine oaths: the LORD's decree enthroning the King at His right hand until every enemy becomes His footstool, verses 1-3, and the LORD's oath establishing Him as a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek, who judges the nations in the day of His wrath, verses 4-7.

verses.text: A Psalm of David. The LORD said to my Lord: "Sit at My right hand until I make Your enemies a footstool for Your feet."
The LORD extends Your mighty scepter from Zion: "Rule in the midst of Your enemies."
Your people shall be willing on Your day of battle. Arrayed in holy splendor, from the womb of the dawn, to You belongs the {{tal|dew}} of Your youth.

units.label: A priest forever after the order of Melchizedek
units.note: the eternal priest-king, judge of the nations

verses.text: The LORD has sworn and will not change His mind: "You are a {{kohen|priest}} forever in the order of {{malkitzedek|Melchizedek}}."
The Lord is at Your right hand; He will crush kings in the day of His wrath.
He will judge the nations, heaping up the dead; He will crush the leaders far and wide.
He will drink from the brook by the road; therefore He will lift up His head.

tal.gloss: Dew, an image of freshness, vitality, and countless abundance appearing at dawn. The King's youthful army is pictured as numerous and vigorous as morning dew, arrayed in holy splendor for His day of battle.

kohen.gloss: Priest, one who mediates between God and people through sacrifice and intercession. This King is declared a priest by divine oath, a role Israel's law kept strictly separate from kingship, making this figure wholly unique.

malkitzedek.gloss: Melchizedek, meaning king of righteousness, the ancient king-priest of Salem who blessed Abraham, Genesis 14:18-20. The psalm declares the coming King a priest forever after this same rare, pre-Levitical order.

basic.context: David, Israel's king, speaks of another figure he calls 'my Lord,' someone greater than himself, whom God invites to sit at His own right hand and whom God swears by oath to be a priest forever after the pattern of the ancient king-priest Melchizedek.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm is Israel's own king confessing a superior King and Priest to come; Jesus later presses exactly this puzzle on the Pharisees, asking how David's own son could also be David's Lord, Matthew 22:41-45, forcing them to reckon with a Messiah who is more than a mere human descendant.
basic.originalLanguages: God invites this King's Lord to sit at His right hand, the place of shared rule and highest honor, while His army is as numerous and vigorous as tal, morning dew. Most strikingly, God swears by oath that this King is also kohen, priest, forever in the order of Melchizedek, malki tzedeq, uniting throne and altar in one person.
basic.typology: Melchizedek, the king-priest of Salem who had no recorded beginning or end of days in the Genesis narrative, Genesis 14:18-20, foreshadows a priesthood not inherited by bloodline but established by God's own unchangeable oath, a pattern the book of Hebrews unfolds at length concerning Christ.
basic.christ: This is the most quoted Old Testament passage in the New Testament. Jesus applies verse 1 to Himself to press His own deity and Davidic Lordship, Matthew 22:41-45; the apostles read His ascension and enthronement as the fulfillment of sitting at God's right hand, Acts 2:34-35, 1 Corinthians 15:25, Hebrews 1:13; and Hebrews 5 through 7 builds its entire argument for Christ's eternal, unbreakable priesthood on verse 4's oath concerning Melchizedek's order.
basic.god: God Himself speaks and swears, installing this King-Priest by sovereign decree and unbreakable oath, not by human election or hereditary right. He shares His throne with this figure, fights for Him against every hostile king, and secures His priesthood forever by an oath He will not revoke.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 22:41-45 records Jesus using this psalm to press His identity as David's Lord. Acts 2:34-35 applies verse 1 to Christ's ascension and enthronement. Hebrews 5:6, 7:17, and 7:21 build the case for Christ's eternal priesthood directly from verse 4.
basic.oneStory: The King David calls his own Lord is revealed across Scripture to be Jesus Christ, enthroned at God's right hand and declared priest forever by unbreakable oath, so that in Him kingship and priesthood, government and mediation, meet in a single person who reigns now and will one day subdue every enemy, including death itself.

context.literary: A royal psalm built on two divine speeches introduced by solemn formulas, the LORD said, verse 1, and the LORD has sworn, verse 4, each followed by a description of the King's reign, His willing army, and His crushing judgment on hostile kings and nations.
context.historical: Likely composed for a Davidic king's enthronement or a related royal occasion, yet its language of a King greater than David, seated at God's own right hand and holding a priesthood outside the Levitical line, points beyond any merely human monarch of Israel's history.
context.canonical: Recognized from antiquity, by both Jewish and Christian readers, as a messianic psalm; it stands as the single most quoted Old Testament chapter in the New Testament, cited or alluded to in the Gospels, Acts, the Epistles, and Hebrews.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To confess, under divine inspiration, a coming King and Priest greater than David himself, installed by God's own decree and oath, whose reign extends over hostile nations and whose priesthood stands outside and above the Levitical order.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm is prophetic confession rather than instruction for conduct; its abiding claim is the identity and twofold office, King and Priest, of the One it describes, now understood by the church to be fulfilled in Jesus Christ.

debates.question: Who is the 'Lord' David addresses in verse 1, someone David calls his superior?

views.label: A future Davidic descendant and king greater than David himself
views.case: The psalm's ancient Near Eastern royal setting fits an oracle spoken over a Davidic king's enthronement, understood messianically as looking toward the ultimate son of David.

views.label: The divine Messiah, whom Jesus Himself argues cannot merely be David's son since David calls Him Lord
views.case: Jesus presses precisely this point in Matthew 22:41-45, using the psalm to show that the Messiah must be more than a human descendant, since David, by the Spirit, calls Him Lord.

debates.bearing: The New Testament resolves the puzzle: the Messiah is both David's son by human descent and David's Lord by divine nature, exactly as Psalm 110 anticipates and Jesus argues.

study.originalLanguages: The LORD invites this King's Lord to sit at His right hand, the seat of shared honor and rule, while His willing people are as countless and vigorous as tal, morning dew. Most strikingly, in verse 4 God swears an unbreakable oath that this King is also kohen, priest, forever after the order of Melchizedek, malki tzedeq, king of righteousness, joining throne and altar in one person.

typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ, whose priesthood, like Melchizedek's, does not depend on Levitical descent but stands forever by God's own oath, unpacked at length in Hebrews 5 through 7.

typology.type: The King seated at God's right hand until His enemies become His footstool, verse 1
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ's ascension and present session at the Father's right hand, Acts 2:34-35, where He reigns until He has put all His enemies under His feet, 1 Corinthians 15:25, Hebrews 1:13.

study.christ: Jesus Himself quotes verse 1 to silence His critics and press His own divine Sonship, since David, by the Spirit, calls his own descendant Lord, Matthew 22:41-45. Peter's first sermon reads Christ's resurrection and ascension as the fulfillment of this enthronement, Acts 2:34-35, and Paul grounds Christ's present reign, until all enemies including death are subdued, on this same verse, 1 Corinthians 15:25, Hebrews 1:13. Just as vitally, the book of Hebrews builds its case for Christ's unique, unending, and effective priesthood directly on verse 4's oath concerning the order of Melchizedek, Hebrews 5 through 7, showing that Jesus is both reigning King and interceding Priest, forever.
study.god: God speaks and swears with sovereign authority, sharing His own throne with this King and establishing His priesthood by an oath He will never revoke. He fights on this King's behalf against every hostile power and secures a reign and a mediation that never end.

crossReferences.note: Jesus quotes this psalm to press the truth that the Messiah is not merely David's son but also David's Lord.
Peter applies verse 1 to Christ's resurrection and ascension to God's right hand.
Grounds Christ's eternal, unbreakable priesthood directly in the oath of verse 4 concerning the order of Melchizedek.

study.oneStory: David confesses a King greater than himself, invited to share God's own throne and sworn in as priest forever after the order of Melchizedek, a figure the whole New Testament identifies as Jesus Christ, who now reigns at the Father's right hand as King and intercedes forever as Priest, until every enemy, even death, is finally put beneath His feet.

sermon.title: The True King
sermon.why: Keller works through Psalm 110:1-7 as the most quoted Old Testament passage in the New Testament, showing how it presents a King who combines excellencies never combined in a single figure, divine and human, strong yet weak, and how Jesus Himself used this psalm to explain who He is.

quiz.q: What does the LORD invite David's Lord to do in verse 1?

options.text: Sit at His right hand until his enemies become a footstool for his feet.
options.note: This is the enthronement the New Testament repeatedly applies to Christ's ascension and reign.

options.text: Go and personally destroy every enemy at once.
options.note: The psalm depicts a period of reigning at God's right hand before enemies are fully subdued, not instant destruction.

options.text: Remain hidden until all opposition disappears on its own.
options.note: The King is seated in a place of active honor and rule, not hidden away.

options.text: Return to Zion to await further instructions.
options.note: The scepter is extended from Zion, verse 2, but the King's own place is at God's right hand, not merely awaiting orders in Zion.

quiz.q: According to verse 4, by what means is this King also declared a priest?

options.text: By the LORD's own unchangeable oath, forever, after the order of Melchizedek.
options.note: This divine oath is the very basis of Hebrews' argument for Christ's eternal priesthood.

options.text: By inheriting the office from the line of Aaron.
options.note: The order named is Melchizedek's, explicitly distinct from the Aaronic, Levitical priesthood.

options.text: By popular vote among the priests of Israel.
options.note: The priesthood is established by God's own oath, not by any human vote or election.

options.text: By a temporary appointment lasting only David's lifetime.
options.note: The text explicitly says forever, not a temporary or lifetime appointment.

quiz.q: How does Jesus Himself use this psalm in Matthew 22:41-45?

options.text: He presses that the Messiah must be more than David's son, since David calls Him Lord.
options.note: Jesus uses verse 1 to show the Messiah's identity exceeds mere human descent from David.

options.text: He denies that the psalm has any messianic meaning.
options.note: Jesus affirms and applies the psalm's messianic significance directly to Himself.

options.text: He uses it to prove His descent from the tribe of Levi.
options.note: The point is precisely the opposite; this priesthood stands outside the Levitical line, after Melchizedek's order.

options.text: He uses it only to criticize the Pharisees' fasting practices.
options.note: The confrontation concerns the identity of the Messiah as David's Lord, not fasting practices.

quiz.q: What does Melchizedek's order signify about the priesthood described in verse 4?

options.text: A priesthood established by God's oath rather than by hereditary Levitical descent.
options.note: Hebrews 7 draws out exactly this contrast between Melchizedek's order and the Levitical priesthood.

options.text: A priesthood limited strictly to offering grain offerings.
options.note: The text does not restrict this priesthood's function in this way; the point is its unique, non-Levitical basis.

options.text: A priesthood that ended when Melchizedek died.
options.note: The point of the typology, developed in Hebrews, is a priesthood without the interruption of hereditary succession.

options.text: A priesthood reserved only for foreign nations, never for Israel's king.
options.note: The psalm applies this very priesthood to Israel's own coming King, not to a foreign nation exclusively.

quiz.q: How do the apostles apply verse 1 concerning Christ's present reign?

options.text: They teach He is now seated at God's right hand, reigning until all His enemies, including death, are subdued.
options.note: Acts 2:34-35, 1 Corinthians 15:25, and Hebrews 1:13 all apply this verse to Christ's present session and ongoing reign.

options.text: They teach the enthronement described here has not yet begun.
options.note: The apostles teach Christ's session at God's right hand began at His ascension and continues now.

options.text: They apply it only to a future earthly throne in Jerusalem, not to Christ's current reign.
options.note: The New Testament applies this to Christ's present heavenly session, not merely to a future earthly throne.

options.text: They apply it exclusively to the future final judgment, with no present significance.
options.note: The apostles teach this reign is already underway now, even as it moves toward a final subduing of every enemy.

questions: David confesses a King greater than himself, one he calls Lord. How does recognizing Jesus as both fully human and fully divine, both son of David and Lord of David, shape the way you worship Him this week?

This King reigns now at God's right hand even while enemies still exist and must be subdued over time. Where do you need to trust that Christ is already reigning, even in a situation that does not yet look fully resolved?

Christ's priesthood, sworn forever after the order of Melchizedek, means His intercession for you never lapses or needs renewal. How does knowing you have a permanent, unfailing Priest change how you approach God in prayer today?
The psalm unites kingship and priesthood, government and mediation, in a single figure. Where in your life do you need both Christ's authority to rule and His priestly mediation to draw near to God?
David's people are described as willing volunteers on the day of battle. What would it look like for you to serve Christ's kingdom as a willing volunteer this week rather than out of mere obligation?

translationNotes.note: The LORD said to my Lord translates two distinct Hebrew words, Yahweh, God's covenant name, and Adonai, Lord, David's title for the superior figure he describes, a distinction Jesus presses in Matthew 22:41-45.
The dew of Your youth pictures the King's people as fresh, numerous, and vigorous as morning dew, gathered willingly on His day of battle, arrayed in holy splendor.

In the order of Melchizedek recalls the king-priest of Salem in Genesis 14:18-20, whose office, unlike the Levitical priesthood, was not established by hereditary descent but stands as a type of a priesthood without beginning or end of days, Hebrews 7:3.
